name: 12-inch cast iron skillet
note: The pan that does almost everything. Holds heat like a battery, sears like a stovetop steakhouse, and costs less than dinner out.

name: 7-quart enamelled dutch oven
note: Bone broth on Sunday, carnitas on Monday, shepherd's pie on Wednesday. The kitchen's slow MVP.

name: 10-inch carbon steel pan
note: Lighter than cast iron, faster to heat, slick once seasoned. The everyday-egg pan.

name: Roasting pan with V-rack
note: Holds a whole bird, drips into the bottom, fond becomes Sunday's gravy.

name: 8-inch chef's knife
note: Sharp matters more than expensive. Buy a good one, learn to hone it, and replace your block set with one tool that works.

name: 6-inch boning knife
note: For breaking down a chicken or trimming silver skin off a tenderloin. Sharp, flexible, narrow.

name: End-grain butcher block
note: Easier on the knife, easier on the eyes. Mineral-oil it once a month and it'll outlive your stove.

name: 10-inch honing rod
note: Two strokes per side, every other use. Keeps the edge true between sharpenings.

name: Instant-read thermometer
note: The single tool that turns guesswork into doneness. Pull at 122°F for medium-rare and stop apologising.

name: Digital kitchen scale
note: Recipes in grams cook the same way every time. Cups don't.

name: Slotted fish spatula
note: Slim, slotted, angled. The right tool for flipping anything that wants to stick.

name: Spider strainer
note: For lifting things out of hot fat. Bigger than a slotted spoon, faster than tongs.

name: Adjustable pepper mill
note: Pre-ground pepper is dust. A mill is the difference between seasoning and not.

name: Charcoal chimney
note: Lights a full chimney of lump charcoal in 12 minutes. No lighter fluid, no waiting, no apologies.

name: Pellet grill, mid-size
note: Set the temp, walk away, come back to dinner. Smoke when you want it, sear when you don't.
